source.title: Neural correlates of cognitive dissonance and choice-induced preference change
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA

confidence_rationale: fMRI study with proper Chen-Risen control (matched non-choice condition), n=20 healthy adults, two convergent measures (self-report and striatum BOLD) of preference change. ACC and dlPFC track dissonance trial-by-trial. Source quality is high (PNAS, peer-reviewed, methodologically defensible against the foundational free-choice critique). Mapping fidelity for the brief is moderate-high: the experiment cleanly demonstrates that a typed choice (the commit) shifts preference and shifts the neural representation of preference, which is the empirical core of commit-boundary belief lock.
failure_signatures: Free-choice paradigm without a non-choice control condition spuriously produces choice-induced preference change even when no true preference shift exists (Chen & Risen 2010); Izuma's design avoids this by using a blind-rating control.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The mere act of making a choice between equally preferred items shifts both self-reported preference and striatal value representation; ACC and dlPFC activity tracks the trial-by-trial degree of cognitive dissonance.
mapping.ree_translation: Direct empirical anchor for the commit-boundary belief lock hypothesis: crossing a typed commit boundary (choice between matched alternatives) causes a measurable, lasting shift in the neural value representation of the choice content. The ACC + dlPFC engagement during dissonance maps to the conflict-resolution machinery that the proposed mechanism predicts will be recruited when revising acted-upon beliefs.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paradigm uses preferences over consumer items, not propositional beliefs about world state; transfer from preference shifts to belief-revision-resistance assumes the same neural machinery handles both, which is plausible but not directly demonstrated. Acted-upon-vs-not asymmetry is implied by the design (no choice = no shift) but not framed as such by the authors.
mapping.source_context: Healthy adults; preference rating + free-choice paradigm with non-choice control; fMRI; Japan.

confidence_components.notes: PNAS, methodologically rigorous (the paper that re-established the validity of the free-choice paradigm post-Chen-Risen). Transfer risk moderate because preference != belief in the strict propositional sense, but the underlying value-update circuitry is the relevant one for the proposed mechanism.
